message.content:
This session is being continued from a previous conversation that ran out of context. The summary below covers the earlier portion of the conversation.

Summary:
1. Primary Request and Intent:
   - "please see our vps files" — explore and understand the CortexBuild VPS project structure
   - "continue working on our project" — resume work on CortexBuild Ultimate, a UK construction management SaaS platform
   - "continue - fix all modules" — fix all frontend modules (TypeScript errors + dark theme issues)
   - "continue" (third session) — continue fixing modules, identified light-theme Tailwind remnants across 28/30 modules
   - "/claude-automation-recommender" — analyze the codebase and recommend Claude Code automations
   - "continue" (fourth/current) — continue with remaining module fixes; currently auditing Settings.tsx to wire hardcoded user data to live API

2. Key Technical Concepts:
   - React 18 + TypeScript (strict mode) + Vite 8 frontend
   - Express.js + PostgreSQL + JWT + bcrypt backend
   - TanStack React Query v5 for data fetching/caching
   - WebSocket (ws module) for real-time notifications
   - nginx reverse proxy serving static dist + proxying /api and /ws
   - PM2 process manager for the Node.js API server
   - PostgreSQL SCRAM-SHA-256 vs MD5 auth — must match between pg_hba.conf and password storage
   - Tailwind CSS dark theme using CSS variables (--slate-*, --amber-*, --emerald-*, --red-*)
   - pg-pool connection pooling in node-postgres
   - TypeScript spread type inference limitations with Record<string, unknown>
   - Lucide-react vs Recharts name collision (PieChart)
   - Parallel agent dispatch for independent file operations

3. Files and Code Sections:
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/server/.env`
     - Contains DB credentials: DB_USER=cortexbuild, DB_PASSWORD=CortexBuild2024!, JWT_SECRET, PORT=3001
   
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/server/db.js`
     - pg Pool connecting via 127.0.0.1 (TCP, not Unix socket) to avoid auth issues
   
   - `/etc/postgresql/16/main/pg_hba.conf`
     - Changed `md5` → `scram-sha-256` for 127.0.0.1/32 to match SCRAM password storage
   
   - `/etc/nginx/sites-available/cortexbuild`
     - Added /ws WebSocket proxy block:
     ```nginx
     location /ws {
         proxy_pass http://127.0.0.1:3001;
         proxy_http_version 1.1;
         proxy_set_header Upgrade $http_upgrade;
         proxy_set_header Connection "upgrade";
         proxy_set_header Host $host;
         proxy_read_timeout 86400;
     }
     ```
   
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/src/hooks/useNotifications.ts`
     - Fixed hardcoded `ws://localhost:3001/ws` to dynamic URL:
     ```typescript
     const WS_URL = (() => {
       const proto = window.location.protocol === 'https:' ? 'wss:' : 'ws:';
       return `${proto}//${window.location.host}/ws`;
     })();
     ```
   
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/vite.config.ts`
     - Added dev proxy for /api, /ws, /uploads:
     ```typescript
     proxy: {
       '/api': { target: 'http://localhost:3001', changeOrigin: true },
       '/ws':  { target: 'ws://localhost:3001', ws: true },
       '/uploads': { target: 'http://localhost:3001', changeOrigin: true },
     }
     ```
   
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/src/components/modules/Insights.tsx`
     - Fixed duplicate PieChart identifier: aliased lucide's `PieChart` → `PieChartIcon`
     - Added `type SeverityKey = keyof typeof SEVERITY;` after the SEVERITY constant
     - Changed `<PieChart style=...>` icon to `<PieChartIcon style=...>`
   
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/src/components/modules/PredictiveAnalytics.tsx`
     - Fixed delayPredictions missing properties by explicitly including project fields + cost prediction data:
     ```typescript
     const delayPredictions = projects
       .filter(p => p.status === 'active')
       .map(p => {
         const cost = predictCostOverrun(p, changeOrders);
         return {
           name: String(p.name ?? ''),
           status: String(p.status ?? ''),
           progress: Number(p.progress ?? 0),
           ...predictDelayRisk(p),
           overrunRisk: cost.overrunRisk,
           varianceAtCompletion: cost.varianceAtCompletion,
         };
       });
     ```
   
   - All 30 `src/components/modules/*.tsx` files
     - Dark theme fixes: replaced `bg-white`, `bg-gray-50`, `bg-gray-100`, `border-gray-200`, `border-gray-300`, `text-gray-900`, `text-gray-800`, `text-gray-700` with dark equivalents
     - Heavy files (CRM, DailyReports, Meetings, Materials, Documents) fixed by parallel agents
     - Lighter files fixed with sed: `bg-white→bg-gray-900`, `bg-gray-100→bg-gray-800`, `border-gray-200→border-gray-700`, `text-gray-900→text-white`, `text-gray-800→text-gray-100`, `text-gray-700→text-gray-300`
     - CRM badge maps specifically fixed to dark variants: `bg-green-500/20 text-green-300` etc.
   
   - `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/src/components/modules/Settings.tsx` (currently being worked on)
     - Has hardcoded 6-user array (lines 40-47) instead of live `usersApi.getAll()`
     - Password change form (line 458) only calls `toast.success()`, not the real API
     - Invite modal (line 267) only calls `toast.success()`, not `usersApi.create()`
     - Role badge colors still use light theme: `bg-red-100 text-red-700` etc. (lines 100-106)
     - Was reading through to line ~325 to understand full scope before rewriting

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **Missing `ws` module**: Server crashed on startup. Fixed by running `npm install ws` in `/var/www/cortexbuild-ultimate/server/`
   
   - **PostgreSQL auth failure (password authentication failed for user "cortexbuild")**: 
     - Root cause: password was stored as SCRAM-SHA-256 (PostgreSQL 16 default) but pg_hba.conf required `md5`
     - node-postgres failed silently while psql succeeded (psql handles both)
     - Fix: Changed pg_hba.conf to `scram-sha-256`, ran `SET password_encryption='scram-sha-256'; ALTER USER cortexbuild WITH PASSWORD 'CortexBuild2024!';`, restarted PostgreSQL
   
   - **TypeScript error - Duplicate identifier 'PieChart' in Insights.tsx**:
     - Both `lucide-react` and `recharts` export `PieChart`
     - Fixed by aliasing lucide import: `PieChart as PieChartIcon`
   
   - **TypeScript error - Cannot find name 'SeverityKey' in Insights.tsx**:
     - Type was used but never declared
     - Fixed by adding `type SeverityKey = keyof typeof SEVERITY;` after the SEVERITY constant
   
   - **TypeScript errors - Missing properties on delayPredictions in PredictiveAnalytics.tsx**:
     - `name`, `status`, `progress` missing because TypeScript loses `Record<string,unknown>` index signature when spreading with a concretely-typed object
     - `overrunRisk`, `varianceAtCompletion` missing because only `predictDelayRisk` was spread, not `predictCostOverrun`
     - Fixed by explicitly mapping required project fields + spreading both prediction functions
   
   - **WebSocket connects to wrong host**: Hardcoded `ws://localhost:3001/ws` means browser connects to client's localhost, not VPS
     - Fixed: dynamic URL derived from `window.location.host`
     - Also added nginx /ws proxy block so WebSocket traffic routes through port 80

5. Problem Solving:
   - **DB connection architecture**: Used 127.0.0.1 (TCP) not localhost (Unix socket) for node-postgres compatibility with pg_hba.conf `host` rules
   - **TypeScript spread inference**: When merging `AnyRow` (Record<string,unknown>) with typed objects, TypeScript drops the index signature. Solution: explicitly enumerate needed string properties
   - **Dark theme audit**: Used `grep -cP` with negative lookahead to distinguish `bg-gray-50` (light, bad) from `bg-gray-500` (dark, fine) — word boundary `\b` would catch `50` but not `500`
   - **Parallel agent strategy**: Dispatched 3 agents simultaneously for the 6 heaviest modules (57+47+46+45+41+29 light-theme hits), reducing wall time ~4x

6. All User Messages:
   - "please see our vps files"
   - "continue working on our project"
   - "continue - fix all modules"
   - "continue"
   - (received `/ralph-loop` with no task — flagged to user that a task is needed)
   - (received `/claude-automation-recommender` — ran the skill)
   - "continue"

7. Pending Tasks:
   - **Settings.tsx live data wiring** (in progress): Replace hardcoded 6-user array with `usersApi.getAll()`, wire invite modal to `usersApi.create()` (requires name + temp password fields), wire delete button to `usersApi.delete()`, wire password change form to `/api/auth/password` endpoint, fix role badge colors from light to dark theme
   - Rebuild and deploy to production after Settings fix

8. Current Work:
   Immediately before this summary, the work was reading through `Settings.tsx` to understand its full structure in preparation for rewriting it to use live API data. Had read through line ~325. The key findings were:
   
   - Lines 40-47: Hardcoded users array (6 fake users — should use `usersApi.getAll()`)
   - Lines 100-106: Light-theme role badge colors:
     ```typescript
     const ROLE_COLOURS: Record<string,string> = {
       super_admin:     'bg-red-100 text-red-700',
       admin:           'bg-orange-100 text-orange-700',
       project_manager: 'bg-blue-100 text-blue-700',
       field_worker:    'bg-green-100 text-green-700',
       client:          'bg-purple-100 text-purple-700',
     };
     ```
   - Line 234: Status badge also light theme: `bg-green-100 text-green-700`
   - Lines 245-273: Invite modal collects only email + role — but `usersApi.create()` needs name + password too
   - Line 267: Invite just calls `toast.success()` — not wired to API
   - Line 237: Delete calls `toast.success()` — not wired to API
   - Lines 446-461: Password change form has 3 separate inputs rendered from a `.map()` with no individual state — not wired to `/api/auth/password`

9. Optional Next Step:
   Continue rewriting `Settings.tsx` to:
   1. Replace hardcoded users with `usersApi.getAll()` (using `useQuery` or direct fetch with `useState`/`useEffect`)
   2. Wire invite form — add `name` and `password` fields, call `usersApi.create()`
   3. Wire delete to `usersApi.delete(id)`
   4. Give the password change form proper state (currentPassword, newPassword, confirmPassword) and wire to `fetch('/api/auth/password', { method: 'PUT', ... })`
   5. Fix `ROLE_COLOURS` and status badge to dark-theme variants
   
   This is directly in line with the current work ("continue" — fixing all modules) and the immediate task that was interrupted by the summary request: reading Settings.tsx in preparation for rewriting the live-data sections.

If you need specific details from before compaction (like exact code snippets, error messages, or content you generated), read the full transcript at: /root/.claude/projects/-root/cd0cc5dd-a173-476d-8100-7ecb3439cb80.jsonl
Continue the conversation from where it left off without asking the user any further questions. Resume directly — do not acknowledge the summary, do not recap what was happening, do not preface with "I'll continue" or similar. Pick up the last task as if the break never happened.

# Verification Before Completion

## Overview

Claiming work is complete without verification is dishonesty, not efficiency.

**Core principle:** Evidence before claims, always.

**Violating the letter of this rule is violating the spirit of this rule.**

## The Iron Law

```
NO COMPLETION CLAIMS WITHOUT FRESH VERIFICATION EVIDENCE
```

If you haven't run the verification command in this message, you cannot claim it passes.

## The Gate Function

```
BEFORE claiming any status or expressing satisfaction:

1. IDENTIFY: What command proves this claim?
2. RUN: Execute the FULL command (fresh, complete)
3. READ: Full output, check exit code, count failures
4. VERIFY: Does output confirm the claim?
   - If NO: State actual status with evidence
   - If YES: State claim WITH evidence
5. ONLY THEN: Make the claim

Skip any step = lying, not verifying
```

## Common Failures

| Claim | Requires | Not Sufficient |
|-------|----------|----------------|
| Tests pass | Test command output: 0 failures | Previous run, "should pass" |
| Linter clean | Linter output: 0 errors | Partial check, extrapolation |
| Build succeeds | Build command: exit 0 | Linter passing, logs look good |
| Bug fixed | Test original symptom: passes | Code changed, assumed fixed |
| Regression test works | Red-green cycle verified | Test passes once |
| Agent completed | VCS diff shows changes | Agent reports "success" |
| Requirements met | Line-by-line checklist | Tests passing |

## Red Flags - STOP

- Using "should", "probably", "seems to"
- Expressing satisfaction before verification ("Great!", "Perfect!", "Done!", etc.)
- About to commit/push/PR without verification
- Trusting agent success reports
- Relying on partial verification
- Thinking "just this once"
- Tired and wanting work over
- **ANY wording implying success without having run verification**

## Rationalization Prevention

| Excuse | Reality |
|--------|---------|
| "Should work now" | RUN the verification |
| "I'm confident" | Confidence ≠ evidence |
| "Just this once" | No exceptions |
| "Linter passed" | Linter ≠ compiler |
| "Agent said success" | Verify independently |
| "I'm tired" | Exhaustion ≠ excuse |
| "Partial check is enough" | Partial proves nothing |
| "Different words so rule doesn't apply" | Spirit over letter |

## Key Patterns

**Tests:**
```
✅ [Run test command] [See: 34/34 pass] "All tests pass"
❌ "Should pass now" / "Looks correct"
```

**Regression tests (TDD Red-Green):**
```
✅ Write → Run (pass) → Revert fix → Run (MUST FAIL) → Restore → Run (pass)
❌ "I've written a regression test" (without red-green verification)
```

**Build:**
```
✅ [Run build] [See: exit 0] "Build passes"
❌ "Linter passed" (linter doesn't check compilation)
```

**Requirements:**
```
✅ Re-read plan → Create checklist → Verify each → Report gaps or completion
❌ "Tests pass, phase complete"
```

**Agent delegation:**
```
✅ Agent reports success → Check VCS diff → Verify changes → Report actual state
❌ Trust agent report
```

## Why This Matters

From 24 failure memories:
- your human partner said "I don't believe you" - trust broken
- Undefined functions shipped - would crash
- Missing requirements shipped - incomplete features
- Time wasted on false completion → redirect → rework
- Violates: "Honesty is a core value. If you lie, you'll be replaced."

## When To Apply

**ALWAYS before:**
- ANY variation of success/completion claims
- ANY expression of satisfaction
- ANY positive statement about work state
- Committing, PR creation, task completion
- Moving to next task
- Delegating to agents

**Rule applies to:**
- Exact phrases
- Paraphrases and synonyms
- Implications of success
- ANY communication suggesting completion/correctness

## The Bottom Line

**No shortcuts for verification.**

Run the command. Read the output. THEN claim the result.

This is non-negotiable.

Endpoint is live and responding correctly. Verification results:

- **TypeScript**: 0 errors
- **Build**: ✓ 2123 modules, built in 1.01s, exit 0
- **Server endpoints**: `GET /api/auth/users`, `POST /api/auth/users`, `DELETE /api/auth/users/:id`, `PUT /api/auth/password` — all confirmed in `auth.js`
- **nginx**: config valid, reloaded cleanly
- **API health**: `/api/auth/users` returns 401 with correct JSON (not a 404)

`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- React Query's `useMutation` with `onSuccess: qc.invalidateQueries()` gives you optimistic-style UX without manual state splicing — the list auto-refreshes after create/delete without any array manipulation code
- The `.map(u => { ... return <tr>; })` pattern (statement body) vs `.map(u => ( <tr> ))` (expression body) matters here because we needed `const` declarations for type narrowing — expression bodies can't contain statements
- Password inputs that share one `showPass` toggle all reveal/hide together, which is the standard UX for a change-password form — but they must have *separate* state refs so each field value is independently tracked before the `fetch` call
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

| Users list | 6 hardcoded rows | `useQuery` → `GET /api/auth/users` |

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- The light-badge pattern (`bg-green-100 text-green-700`) is Tailwind's default approach for light-mode UI — on a dark background it creates jarring white islands. The dark equivalent (`bg-green-500/20 text-green-300`) uses the same hue but at 20% opacity background with a lighter text — giving the same visual hierarchy without the contrast clash
- The sed loop over COLOR values is much more robust than one-shot regex chains — iterating over `green blue red yellow orange purple` guarantees full coverage rather than relying on a single complex regex that can silently miss variants like `text-green-800` vs `text-green-700`
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

 created_at  | timestamp with time zone |           |          | now()
Indexes:
    "tenders_pkey" PRIMARY KEY, btree (id)
Check constraints:
    "tenders_ai_score_check" CHECK (ai_score >= 0 AND ai_score <= 100)
    "tenders_probability_check" CHECK (probability >= 0 AND probability <= 100)
    "tenders_status_check" CHECK (status = ANY (ARRAY['drafting'::text, 'submitted'::text, 'shortlisted'::text, 'won'::text, 'lost'::text]))

content.text:
`★ Insight ─────────────────────────────────────`
- PostgreSQL's `CHECK` constraints on ENUM-like columns are discovered at insert time, not schema read time — always run `\d tablename` to find them before bulk inserting. The `documents.category_check` only allows uppercase values (`PLANS`, `DRAWINGS`, etc.) which isn't obvious from the column name
- Seed data quality has a big impact on first impressions of a SaaS demo: modules showing "No records found" look broken, while populated modules show off the actual UI design and data relationships
- Daily report `temperature` being `text` not `integer` means `'9°C'` is valid — but the API's column whitelist in `generic.js` controls what gets written, and `\d` is the authoritative source for column types
`─────────────────────────────────────────────────`

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /root/.claude/plugins/cache/claude-plugins-official/superpowers/5.0.5/skills/systematic-debugging

# Systematic Debugging

## Overview

Random fixes waste time and create new bugs. Quick patches mask underlying issues.

**Core principle:** ALWAYS find root cause before attempting fixes. Symptom fixes are failure.

**Violating the letter of this process is violating the spirit of debugging.**

## The Iron Law

```
NO FIXES WITHOUT ROOT CAUSE INVESTIGATION FIRST
```

If you haven't completed Phase 1, you cannot propose fixes.

## When to Use

Use for ANY technical issue:
- Test failures
- Bugs in production
- Unexpected behavior
- Performance problems
- Build failures
- Integration issues

**Use this ESPECIALLY when:**
- Under time pressure (emergencies make guessing tempting)
- "Just one quick fix" seems obvious
- You've already tried multiple fixes
- Previous fix didn't work
- You don't fully understand the issue

**Don't skip when:**
- Issue seems simple (simple bugs have root causes too)
- You're in a hurry (rushing guarantees rework)
- Manager wants it fixed NOW (systematic is faster than thrashing)

## The Four Phases

You MUST complete each phase before proceeding to the next.

### Phase 1: Root Cause Investigation

**BEFORE attempting ANY fix:**

1. **Read Error Messages Carefully**
   - Don't skip past errors or warnings
   - They often contain the exact solution
   - Read stack traces completely
   - Note line numbers, file paths, error codes

2. **Reproduce Consistently**
   - Can you trigger it reliably?
   - What are the exact steps?
   - Does it happen every time?
   - If not reproducible → gather more data, don't guess

3. **Check Recent Changes**
   - What changed that could cause this?
   - Git diff, recent commits
   - New dependencies, config changes
   - Environmental differences

4. **Gather Evidence in Multi-Component Systems**

   **WHEN system has multiple components (CI → build → signing, API → service → database):**

   **BEFORE proposing fixes, add diagnostic instrumentation:**
   ```
   For EACH component boundary:
     - Log what data enters component
     - Log what data exits component
     - Verify environment/config propagation
     - Check state at each layer

   Run once to gather evidence showing WHERE it breaks
   THEN analyze evidence to identify failing component
   THEN investigate that specific component
   ```

   **Example (multi-layer system):**
   ```bash
   # Layer 1: Workflow
   echo "=== Secrets available in workflow: ==="
   echo "IDENTITY: ${IDENTITY:+SET}${IDENTITY:-UNSET}"

   # Layer 2: Build script
   echo "=== Env vars in build script: ==="
   env | grep IDENTITY || echo "IDENTITY not in environment"

   # Layer 3: Signing script
   echo "=== Keychain state: ==="
   security list-keychains
   security find-identity -v

   # Layer 4: Actual signing
   codesign --sign "$IDENTITY" --verbose=4 "$APP"
   ```

   **This reveals:** Which layer fails (secrets → workflow ✓, workflow → build ✗)

5. **Trace Data Flow**

   **WHEN error is deep in call stack:**

   See `root-cause-tracing.md` in this directory for the complete backward tracing technique.

   **Quick version:**
   - Where does bad value originate?
   - What called this with bad value?
   - Keep tracing up until you find the source
   - Fix at source, not at symptom

### Phase 2: Pattern Analysis

**Find the pattern before fixing:**

1. **Find Working Examples**
   - Locate similar working code in same codebase
   - What works that's similar to what's broken?

2. **Compare Against References**
   - If implementing pattern, read reference implementation COMPLETELY
   - Don't skim - read every line
   - Understand the pattern fully before applying

3. **Identify Differences**
   - What's different between working and broken?
   - List every difference, however small
   - Don't assume "that can't matter"

4. **Understand Dependencies**
   - What other components does this need?
   - What settings, config, environment?
   - What assumptions does it make?

### Phase 3: Hypothesis and Testing

**Scientific method:**

1. **Form Single Hypothesis**
   - State clearly: "I think X is the root cause because Y"
   - Write it down
   - Be specific, not vague

2. **Test Minimally**
   - Make the SMALLEST possible change to test hypothesis
   - One variable at a time
   - Don't fix multiple things at once

3. **Verify Before Continuing**
   - Did it work? Yes → Phase 4
   - Didn't work? Form NEW hypothesis
   - DON'T add more fixes on top

4. **When You Don't Know**
   - Say "I don't understand X"
   - Don't pretend to know
   - Ask for help
   - Research more

### Phase 4: Implementation

**Fix the root cause, not the symptom:**

1. **Create Failing Test Case**
   - Simplest possible reproduction
   - Automated test if possible
   - One-off test script if no framework
   - MUST have before fixing
   - Use the `superpowers:test-driven-development` skill for writing proper failing tests

2. **Implement Single Fix**
   - Address the root cause identified
   - ONE change at a time
   - No "while I'm here" improvements
   - No bundled refactoring

3. **Verify Fix**
   - Test passes now?
   - No other tests broken?
   - Issue actually resolved?

4. **If Fix Doesn't Work**
   - STOP
   - Count: How many fixes have you tried?
   - If < 3: Return to Phase 1, re-analyze with new information
   - **If ≥ 3: STOP and question the architecture (step 5 below)**
   - DON'T attempt Fix #4 without architectural discussion

5. **If 3+ Fixes Failed: Question Architecture**

   **Pattern indicating architectural problem:**
   - Each fix reveals new shared state/coupling/problem in different place
   - Fixes require "massive refactoring" to implement
   - Each fix creates new symptoms elsewhere

   **STOP and question fundamentals:**
   - Is this pattern fundamentally sound?
   - Are we "sticking with it through sheer inertia"?
   - Should we refactor architecture vs. continue fixing symptoms?

   **Discuss with your human partner before attempting more fixes**

   This is NOT a failed hypothesis - this is a wrong architecture.

## Red Flags - STOP and Follow Process

If you catch yourself thinking:
- "Quick fix for now, investigate later"
- "Just try changing X and see if it works"
- "Add multiple changes, run tests"
- "Skip the test, I'll manually verify"
- "It's probably X, let me fix that"
- "I don't fully understand but this might work"
- "Pattern says X but I'll adapt it differently"
- "Here are the main problems: [lists fixes without investigation]"
- Proposing solutions before tracing data flow
- **"One more fix attempt" (when already tried 2+)**
- **Each fix reveals new problem in different place**

**ALL of these mean: STOP. Return to Phase 1.**

**If 3+ fixes failed:** Question the architecture (see Phase 4.5)

## your human partner's Signals You're Doing It Wrong

**Watch for these redirections:**
- "Is that not happening?" - You assumed without verifying
- "Will it show us...?" - You should have added evidence gathering
- "Stop guessing" - You're proposing fixes without understanding
- "Ultrathink this" - Question fundamentals, not just symptoms
- "We're stuck?" (frustrated) - Your approach isn't working

**When you see these:** STOP. Return to Phase 1.

## Common Rationalizations

| Excuse | Reality |
|--------|---------|
| "Issue is simple, don't need process" | Simple issues have root causes too. Process is fast for simple bugs. |
| "Emergency, no time for process" | Systematic debugging is FASTER than guess-and-check thrashing. |
| "Just try this first, then investigate" | First fix sets the pattern. Do it right from the start. |
| "I'll write test after confirming fix works" | Untested fixes don't stick. Test first proves it. |
| "Multiple fixes at once saves time" | Can't isolate what worked. Causes new bugs. |
| "Reference too long, I'll adapt the pattern" | Partial understanding guarantees bugs. Read it completely. |
| "I see the problem, let me fix it" | Seeing symptoms ≠ understanding root cause. |
| "One more fix attempt" (after 2+ failures) | 3+ failures = architectural problem. Question pattern, don't fix again. |

## Quick Reference

| Phase | Key Activities | Success Criteria |
|-------|---------------|------------------|
| **1. Root Cause** | Read errors, reproduce, check changes, gather evidence | Understand WHAT and WHY |
| **2. Pattern** | Find working examples, compare | Identify differences |
| **3. Hypothesis** | Form theory, test minimally | Confirmed or new hypothesis |
| **4. Implementation** | Create test, fix, verify | Bug resolved, tests pass |

## When Process Reveals "No Root Cause"

If systematic investigation reveals issue is truly environmental, timing-dependent, or external:

1. You've completed the process
2. Document what you investigated
3. Implement appropriate handling (retry, timeout, error message)
4. Add monitoring/logging for future investigation

**But:** 95% of "no root cause" cases are incomplete investigation.

## Supporting Techniques

These techniques are part of systematic debugging and available in this directory:

- **`root-cause-tracing.md`** - Trace bugs backward through call stack to find original trigger
- **`defense-in-depth.md`** - Add validation at multiple layers after finding root cause
- **`condition-based-waiting.md`** - Replace arbitrary timeouts with condition polling

**Related skills:**
- **superpowers:test-driven-development** - For creating failing test case (Phase 4, Step 1)
- **superpowers:verification-before-completion** - Verify fix worked before claiming success

## Real-World Impact

From debugging sessions:
- Systematic approach: 15-30 minutes to fix
- Random fixes approach: 2-3 hours of thrashing
- First-time fix rate: 95% vs 40%
- New bugs introduced: Near zero vs common

1. Continue fixing modules (dark theme + TypeScript errors)

4. CORS configuration
5. Comprehensive DB seeding
6. Registration endpoint added to server
7. AuthContext.signUp() wired to real API
8. Git commit + push to GitHub

Errors encountered:
- TypeScript regex in sed command was wrong (70[78] instead of 700) - fixed by iterating over colors with exact string matching
- Daily reports INSERT failed because activities/materials/equipment/issues are jsonb, not text - fixed by using JSON string values
- Documents INSERT failed due to CHECK constraint on category (must be uppercase: 'PLANS', 'DRAWINGS', etc.) - fixed by using correct values
- Previous session errors: duplicate PieChart identifier, missing SeverityKey type, TypeScript spread issues

Files modified/created:
- server/routes/auth.js - added POST /api/auth/register
- src/context/AuthContext.tsx - wired signUp to register endpoint
- src/components/modules/Settings.tsx - full live data wiring

- /etc/nginx/sites-available/cortexbuild - HTTPS + domain
- server/.env - CORS_ORIGIN locked to domain
- DB seed data added to 8 thin tables
</analysis>

     router.post('/register', async (req, res) => {
       const { name, email, password, company, phone } = req.body;
       if (!name || !email || !password || !company)

       if (password.length < 8)
         return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Password must be at least 8 characters' });
       const emailRe = /^[^\s@]+@[^\s@]+\.[^\s@]+$/;
       if (!emailRe.test(email))
         return res.status(400).json({ message: 'Invalid email address' });
       try {
         const existing = await pool.query('SELECT id FROM users WHERE email = $1', [email.toLowerCase().trim()]);
         if (existing.rows.length)
           return res.status(409).json({ message: 'An account with that email already exists' });
         const hash = await bcrypt.hash(password, 10);
         const { rows } = await pool.query(
           `INSERT INTO users (name, email, password_hash, role, company, phone)
            VALUES ($1, $2, $3, 'company_owner', $4, $5)
            RETURNING id, name, email, role, company, phone, created_at`,
           [name.trim(), email.toLowerCase().trim(), hash, company.trim(), phone || null]
         );
         const newUser = rows[0];
         const token = jwt.sign(
           { id: newUser.id, email: newUser.email, role: newUser.role, name: newUser.name, company: newUser.company },
           SECRET, { expiresIn: '7d' }
         );
         res.status(201).json({ token, user: newUser });
       } catch (err) { ... }
     });

         method: 'POST',
         headers: { 'Content-Type': 'application/json' },
         body: JSON.stringify({ name, email, password, company }),

       if (!res.ok) throw new Error(data.message || 'Registration failed');

       setStoredUser(data.user);

     };

4. Errors and Fixes:
   - **sed regex `70[78]` didn't match `700`**: The pattern `bg-green-100 text-green-70[78]` was trying to match Tailwind classes ending in `700` or `800` but `70[78]` matches `707`/`708`, not `700`. Fixed by iterating over each color name and using exact string substitution: `bg-${COLOR}-100 text-${COLOR}-700` and `bg-${COLOR}-100 text-${COLOR}-800` separately.
   - **Daily reports INSERT failed — jsonb columns**: `activities`, `materials`, `equipment`, `issues` are `jsonb`, not `text`. First attempt passed plain text strings. Fixed by wrapping values as JSON arrays: `'["item1","item2"]'`
   - **Documents INSERT failed — CHECK constraint**: `documents.category` has constraint: `CHECK (category = ANY (ARRAY['PLANS','DRAWINGS','PERMITS','RAMS','CONTRACTS','REPORTS','SPECS','PHOTOS']))` — all uppercase. First attempt used mixed case like `'Structural'`. Fixed by using correct uppercase values.
   - **Playwright browser sandbox error**: Running as root without `--no-sandbox` not supported. Could not visually verify the UI — verified via curl and direct code review instead.
   - **Root cause of "cannot login"**: `POST /api/auth/register` did not exist in `auth.js`. The request fell through to `app.use('/api', authMiddleware)` at line 31 of `index.js`, returning "No token provided". Additionally, `AuthContext.signUp()` was hardcoded to throw. Fixed by adding the register endpoint and wiring the frontend.
